refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating worksite mission inputs into a set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, etc.). This is solution-neutral and atomic.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints, equipment class), the system produces a documented set of performance targets that are traceable to those inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, baseline-capable).
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall accept worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies, equipment class).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall compute performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy the worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a record of derived performance targets and their source context for traceability.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall support baseline and calibration updates to performance targets with version history.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a parameterized mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational inputs (safety plan directives, mission objectives, environmental constraints) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission context (safety policies, operational modes, hazard classifications) into quantified performance targets via a declarative mapping layer, with traceability links and baseline versioning.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a hierarchical decomposition process that breaks worksite mission context into functional domains (safety, reliability, throughput, precision), derives domain-specific performance metrics via stakeholder-driven workshops, and maintains a configuration-controlled baseline repository.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Create a federated data model that normalizes worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental telemetry) into a common semantic schema, applies transformation rules to generate performance targets, and logs all derivation decisions with source traceability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission context (safety policies, operational modes, hazard classifications) into quantified performance targets via a declarative mapping layer, with traceability links and baseline versioning.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between rule definition and performance target schema—mitigated by separating rule syntax from execution engine.
Dependency on worksite input data quality and completeness—mitigated by explicit validation gates and fallback defaults.
Versioning of rules and baselines must remain independent of operational deployment—mitigated by immutable baseline snapshots and audit logs.
coupling_check.notes: The rule-based approach decouples mission context interpretation (domain logic) from performance target generation (inference), allowing independent evolution of rules and targets. Declarative mapping supports transparency and auditability. Baseline versioning is orthogonal to rule execution, reducing coupling to deployment cycles.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are available in machine-readable or readily formalized form. Risk: If inputs are unstructured or ambiguous, rule derivation may require manual interpretation, introducing latency and subjectivity. Mitigation: Define a structured input template and validation checklist.

Assumption: Performance target derivation rules are stable and can be codified. Risk: If mission context is highly dynamic or novel, pre-defined rules may not cover all scenarios. Mitigation: Include a fallback mechanism for manual override with documented justification.

Assumption: Traceability from worksite inputs to performance targets can be maintained at acceptable overhead. Risk: Audit trail complexity may grow with rule depth and baseline history. Mitigation: Implement incremental logging and periodic archive/summarization.

Assumption: Stakeholders can agree on a common performance target schema. Risk: Divergent interpretations of 'safety,' 'reliability,' or 'precision' may cause disputes. Mitigation: Establish a glossary and reference baseline early in the engagement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive quantified performance requirements from worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) via a declarative rule-based mapping, produce a documented baseline of performance targets with full traceability to source inputs, and maintain a versioned history of all baseline updates and calibration decisions with audit logs. Acceptance criteria: (1) Every performance target is linked to at least one source input via a documented rule. (2) Baseline snapshots are immutable and timestamped. (3) Rule changes and target updates are logged with justification and approval records. (4) System supports rollback to prior baselines and comparison of successive versions.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission context (safety policies, operational modes, hazard classifications) into quantified performance targets with declarative mapping, traceability, and baseline versioning.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context, operational modes, and worksite parameters that feed the inference engine

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outputs quantified performance targets and safety constraints derived from rule inference

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives hazard classifications, environmental state, and operational telemetry to inform rule evaluation

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: rule-based inference, safety policy mapping, and performance target generation are core safety domain responsibilities. Declarative mapping and versioning align with safety service architecture. Minor diffusion: traceability links may require light coupling to Tasking for context provenance.

Overly broad boundary. NGHE Solution is a system-level container; placing inference engine here would diffuse ownership and make safety logic opaque to dedicated safety services. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Vehicle module is for platform-specific control and actuation. Rule-based inference for safety policies is not a vehicle-level concern. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to understand mission context and safety policies, violating domain boundaries.

Weak fit. Teleoperation handles operator-vehicle interaction and command translation. Rule-based safety inference is orthogonal to teleoperation logic. Placing it here would couple safety policy logic to operator interface, reducing reusability and testability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of a rule-based inference engine that translates safety policies and hazard classifications into performance targets. This module already owns safety constraint logic and alert generation; adding declarative rule mapping and baseline versioning extends its core responsibility without introducing cross-cutting concerns. The engine becomes a reusable safety policy interpreter accessible to all other modules via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces.
containment_risks: Traceability links between mission context (Tasking) and inferred targets may require bidirectional coupling; mitigate by defining a lightweight traceability schema passed through SafetyAlert.

Baseline versioning of rules and policies requires persistent storage; ensure Safety Services has access to a configuration/versioning backend without creating tight coupling to a specific database module.
Rule complexity may grow over time; establish clear governance for rule authoring and validation to prevent Safety Services from becoming a monolithic policy repository.
Real-time performance of inference engine under high-frequency telemetry updates; validate latency requirements and consider caching or incremental evaluation strategies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and baseline history. Safety Services module boundary explicitly owns rule-based inference, declarative mapping, and baseline versioning. Traceability can be verified through SafetyAlert output schema and configuration audit logs. Baseline versioning is a core Safety Services responsibility with clear ownership.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-NGHE-005 (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context) maps unambiguously to Safety Services via DP-NGHE-005.2 (rule-based inference engine). The module owns the complete inference pipeline: ingests mission context (Tasking), applies declarative rules, outputs quantified targets (SafetyAlert). No competing allocations identified across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution containers.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.2 directly addresses FR-NGHE-005. The rule-based inference engine with declarative mapping layer is a sound logical solution for translating worksite mission context (safety policies, operational modes, hazard classifications) into quantified performance targets. Traceability links and baseline versioning satisfy acceptance criteria for documented targets and calibration history. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Traceability provenance: Traceability links between Tasking inputs and inferred targets require bidirectional context flow. Mitigate by embedding a lightweight traceability schema (mission_context_id, rule_set_version, timestamp) in SafetyAlert outputs. Avoid tight coupling by treating Tasking as a read-only input source.

Baseline versioning persistence: Rule and policy baseline storage requires access to a configuration/versioning backend. Establish a clear interface contract (e.g., ConfigStore) to decouple Safety Services from specific database implementations. Document version schema and rollback semantics.

Rule complexity growth: As safety policies accumulate, the inference engine risks becoming a monolithic policy repository. Establish governance: define rule authoring templates, validation gates, and periodic rule audits. Consider modularizing rules by hazard class or operational mode to maintain clarity.

Real-time inference latency: High-frequency telemetry updates (Telemetry interface) may stress inference engine performance. Validate latency SLAs against mission-critical alert response times. Consider caching rule evaluation results or incremental evaluation strategies if latency exceeds thresholds.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent fit (0.92 containment score). Safety Services is the natural owner: rule-based inference, safety policy mapping, and performance target generation are core safety domain responsibilities. Declarative mapping and versioning align with safety service architecture. The module boundary is well-defined with three external interfaces (Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Telemetry in) that cleanly separate concerns. Minor diffusion risk is limited to traceability provenance, which is manageable through schema design. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or competing allocations.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the traceability schema: Define a TraceabilityContext data structure (mission_context_id, rule_set_version, evaluation_timestamp, hazard_classification_source) that flows through SafetyAlert outputs. This satisfies acceptance criteria for traceability without introducing bidirectional coupling.

Document the ConfigStore interface: Specify the contract for baseline versioning (get_rule_baseline(version_id), list_baselines(), commit_baseline(rules, metadata)). This decouples Safety Services from database implementation details.

Establish rule governance: Define a rule authoring template (condition, action, hazard_class, operational_mode, validation_status) and a lightweight review process. Document rule lifecycle (draft, validated, active, deprecated) to prevent unbounded complexity growth.

Validate inference latency: Measure end-to-end latency from Telemetry input to SafetyAlert output under peak telemetry frequency. If latency exceeds mission-critical thresholds (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical alerts), implement caching or incremental evaluation strategies and document performance trade-offs.
Add acceptance test: Verify that performance targets output by Safety Services are traceable to worksite inputs via the TraceabilityContext and that baseline history is queryable and auditable.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-005 passes all three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) with high verification feasibility and clear allocation to Safety Services. However, the gate analysis identifies four material diffusion risks that require decomposition into sub-FRs to manage architectural coherence and safety criticality. The recommended revisions—traceability schema formalization, ConfigStore interface specification, rule governance, and latency validation—are not minor implementation details but rather architectural concerns that warrant explicit functional requirements at the next level. Given high safety criticality, decomposing these concerns into sub-FRs ensures they receive rigorous design scrutiny and traceability rather than being deferred to implementation. This approach maintains the principle that safety-critical systems require explicit functional treatment of non-functional concerns (latency, versioning, governance) that could otherwise diffuse across module boundaries.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: Establish bidirectional traceability between worksite mission context inputs (Tasking) and inferred performance targets (SafetyAlert outputs) such that any performance target can be traced to its originating mission context, rule set version, and evaluation timestamp.

FR-NGHE-005.2: Manage baseline versioning and rollback of safety rules and policies with queryable audit history, supporting version comparison, rollback semantics, and configuration provenance tracking.

FR-NGHE-005.3: Govern safety rule authoring, validation, and lifecycle management to prevent unbounded complexity growth and ensure rules remain auditable, maintainable, and aligned with hazard classifications and operational modes.

FR-NGHE-005.4: Ensure inference engine latency from telemetry input to safety alert output meets mission-critical response time thresholds (acceptance criteria: <100ms for safety-critical alerts under peak telemetry frequency).

revise.why: DP-NGHE-005.2 (rule-based inference engine with declarative mapping) is logically sound but underspecifies the architectural mechanisms required to manage the four identified diffusion risks. Revise DP-NGHE-005.2 to explicitly include: (1) TraceabilityContext data structure embedded in SafetyAlert outputs, (2) ConfigStore interface contract for baseline versioning, (3) rule governance framework (authoring template, lifecycle states, review gates), and (4) latency monitoring and caching strategy. These revisions elevate the DP from a conceptual inference engine to an architecturally complete design parameter that addresses safety criticality and module boundary coherence.

assumptions: Mission-critical safety alert response time threshold is <100ms; this assumption must be validated against actual worksite operational requirements.
Tasking inputs are treated as read-only by Safety Services; bidirectional coupling is avoided by embedding traceability context in outputs rather than querying back to Tasking.
Rule complexity growth is anticipated and will be managed through governance; unbounded rule accumulation is a credible risk given the open-ended nature of safety policy evolution.
ConfigStore is a logical abstraction; the actual implementation (relational database, configuration management system, or version control) is deferred to IM layer.

Traceability schema (mission_context_id, rule_set_version, evaluation_timestamp, hazard_classification_source) is sufficient to satisfy acceptance criteria for documented targets and calibration history.
open_risks: Traceability provenance: Bidirectional context flow between Tasking and Safety Services could introduce tight coupling if not carefully managed through schema design. Risk: traceability links become stale or inconsistent if Tasking context evolves independently.

Baseline versioning persistence: ConfigStore interface contract must be formalized before implementation to avoid ad-hoc database design. Risk: versioning semantics (rollback, branching, conflict resolution) are ambiguous, leading to data corruption or audit failures.

Rule complexity growth: Safety policies may accumulate faster than governance processes can manage. Risk: inference engine becomes a monolithic policy repository, reducing maintainability and increasing validation burden.

Real-time inference latency: High-frequency telemetry updates may exceed latency thresholds, especially if rule evaluation is not optimized. Risk: safety alerts are delayed, compromising mission-critical response times.

Rule authoring governance: Lightweight review process may be insufficient for safety-critical rules. Risk: invalid or contradictory rules are deployed, leading to incorrect performance targets or safety violations.
evidence_needed: Worksite operational requirements document specifying mission-critical alert response time thresholds and peak telemetry frequency.
Safety policy inventory and hazard classification taxonomy to validate rule governance framework and identify rule authoring templates.
Baseline versioning use cases (e.g., rollback scenarios, version comparison, audit queries) to inform ConfigStore interface design.
Latency measurement data from prototype or simulation of inference engine under peak telemetry load to validate <100ms threshold feasibility.
Traceability schema validation: demonstrate that TraceabilityContext captures sufficient provenance to satisfy acceptance criteria for documented targets and calibration history.
Rule lifecycle governance documentation: define authoring template, validation gates, and review process; demonstrate that governance prevents unbounded complexity growth.
